nodes.label: Symbolic AI
nodes.summary: The classical AI paradigm based on logic, rules, and explicit knowledge representation. Dominated from the 1950s through the 1980s.

nodes.label: Connectionist / Neural Networks
nodes.summary: AI through networks of artificial neurons that learn from data. From perceptrons (1958) to transformers (2017), the dominant paradigm since 2012.

nodes.label: Probabilistic AI
nodes.summary: Methods grounded in probability theory and statistical inference. Bayesian reasoning, graphical models, and uncertainty quantification.

nodes.label: Evolutionary Computation
nodes.summary: Optimization algorithms inspired by biological evolution: selection, mutation, crossover. Includes genetic algorithms, swarm intelligence, and neuroevolution.

nodes.label: Reinforcement Learning
nodes.summary: Learning through interaction with an environment to maximize cumulative reward. From Q-learning to AlphaGo to RLHF.

nodes.label: Generative Models
nodes.summary: Models that learn to generate new data: images, text, audio, video. GANs, VAEs, diffusion models, and large language models.

nodes.label: Applied AI
nodes.summary: Real-world applications of AI and ML across industries: computer vision, NLP, robotics, healthcare, autonomous systems.

nodes.label: Foundations & History
nodes.summary: The key historical milestones, paradigm shifts, and inflection points that shaped AI as a field from 1950 to present.

nodes.label: Turing Test
nodes.summary: Alan Turing's 1950 paper 'Computing Machinery and Intelligence' proposed the imitation game as a test for machine intelligence, founding the philosophical basis for AI.

nodes.label: Dartmouth Conference (1956)
nodes.summary: The 1956 Dartmouth workshop coined the term 'Artificial Intelligence' and launched AI as a formal academic discipline. Organized by McCarthy, Minsky, Rochester, and Shannon.

nodes.label: Perceptron (1958)
nodes.summary: Frank Rosenblatt's 1958 perceptron was the first trainable neural network. Minsky and Papert's 1969 critique of its limitations contributed to the first AI winter.

nodes.label: First AI Winter (1974-1980)
nodes.summary: Triggered by the 1973 Lighthill Report and DARPA funding cuts in 1974. Perceptron limitations and unfulfilled promises led to a collapse of AI research funding.

nodes.label: Expert Systems Era (1980s)
nodes.summary: Revival between the two AI winters. Rule-based expert systems like XCON saved DEC $40M. By 1985, corporations spent over $1B on AI annually.

nodes.label: Second AI Winter (1987-1993)
nodes.summary: Collapse of the LISP machine market in 1987 as cheaper workstations emerged. Expert systems proved brittle and expensive to maintain, eroding confidence in AI.

nodes.label: Statistical Turn (1990s)
nodes.summary: AI shifted from hand-crafted rules to data-driven statistical methods in the 1990s. Machine learning, SVMs, and probabilistic models replaced symbolic approaches.

nodes.label: Deep Learning Revolution (2012)
nodes.summary: AlexNet's 2012 ImageNet victory proved deep neural networks could dramatically outperform traditional methods, igniting the modern deep learning era.

nodes.label: GOFAI (Good Old-Fashioned AI)
nodes.summary: John Haugeland's term for classical symbolic AI: intelligence as formal symbol manipulation. Dominated the field from the 1950s to the 1980s.

nodes.label: Logic Programming
nodes.summary: Programming paradigm based on formal logic. Prolog (1972) became the primary language. Japan's Fifth Generation Computer project (1982) was built on it.

nodes.label: Expert Systems
nodes.summary: Rule-based systems encoding domain expert knowledge. MYCIN (1976) diagnosed infections; XCON (1980) configured DEC computers. Peak popularity in the 1980s.

nodes.label: Knowledge Representation
nodes.summary: Methods for encoding information about the world in forms that AI systems can reason about: semantic networks, ontologies, description logics.

nodes.label: Automated Planning
nodes.summary: AI systems that generate sequences of actions to achieve goals. STRIPS (1971) introduced the classical planning formalism still used today.

nodes.label: Symbolic NLP
nodes.summary: Early natural language processing using grammars, parse trees, and hand-crafted rules. Chomsky's formal grammars and Winograd's SHRDLU (1970) exemplified this approach.

nodes.label: Ontologies
nodes.summary: Formal specifications of shared conceptualizations. Cyc (1984) attempted to encode all common sense knowledge. OWL became the standard for the Semantic Web.

nodes.label: Semantic Web
nodes.summary: Tim Berners-Lee's vision (2001) of a machine-readable web using RDF, OWL, and SPARQL. A modern evolution of symbolic knowledge representation ideas.

nodes.label: Automated Reasoning
nodes.summary: Using formal logic to automatically prove theorems and verify programs. Resolution theorem proving (1965) and SAT solvers underpin modern verification tools.

nodes.label: Frames
nodes.summary: Minsky's 1974 frame theory: knowledge as stereotyped situations with default values. Influenced object-oriented programming and modern knowledge graphs.

nodes.label: Perceptrons
nodes.summary: Single-layer neural networks that learn linear decision boundaries. Rosenblatt (1958) built the Mark I Perceptron hardware. Limited to linearly separable problems.

nodes.label: Backpropagation (1986)
nodes.summary: Rumelhart, Hinton, and Williams popularized backpropagation in 1986, enabling training of multi-layer networks. The algorithm that made deep learning possible.

nodes.label: Multilayer Perceptron (MLP)
nodes.summary: Feedforward networks with one or more hidden layers. The Universal Approximation Theorem (1989) proved MLPs can approximate any continuous function.

nodes.label: Convolutional Neural Networks
nodes.summary: Networks using convolution operations to extract spatial features. Inspired by the visual cortex. Revolutionized computer vision from LeNet (1998) to ResNet (2015).

nodes.label: LeNet (1998)
nodes.summary: Yann LeCun's 1998 CNN for handwritten digit recognition. Deployed in ATMs nationwide. Proved convolutions could learn hierarchical visual features.

nodes.label: AlexNet (2012)
nodes.summary: Alex Krizhevsky's deep CNN won ImageNet 2012 by a massive margin (top-5 error 15.3% vs 26.2%). Used GPUs, ReLU, and dropout. Ignited the deep learning revolution.

nodes.label: ResNet (2015)
nodes.summary: Kaiming He's residual networks introduced skip connections, enabling training of 152-layer networks. Achieved 3.57% top-5 error, surpassing human performance on ImageNet.

nodes.label: Recurrent Neural Networks
nodes.summary: Networks with feedback connections for processing sequences. Capable of modeling temporal dependencies but prone to vanishing gradients in long sequences.

nodes.label: LSTM (1997)
nodes.summary: Hochreiter and Schmidhuber's 1997 Long Short-Term Memory solved the vanishing gradient problem with gated memory cells. Dominated sequence modeling for 20 years.

nodes.label: GRU (2014)
nodes.summary: Kyunghyun Cho's 2014 Gated Recurrent Unit simplified LSTM with fewer parameters (two gates instead of three) while achieving comparable performance.

nodes.label: Attention Mechanism (2014)
nodes.summary: Bahdanau et al. (2014) introduced attention for neural machine translation, allowing models to dynamically focus on relevant input parts. The key insight behind transformers.

nodes.label: Transformer Architecture (2017)
nodes.summary: Vaswani et al.'s 'Attention Is All You Need' (2017) replaced recurrence with self-attention. Enabled massive parallelization and scaling, becoming the foundation of modern AI.

nodes.label: BERT (2018)
nodes.summary: Google's Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (2018). Pre-trained on masked language modeling, it set new benchmarks across 11 NLP tasks.

nodes.label: GPT-1 (2018)
nodes.summary: OpenAI's first Generative Pre-trained Transformer (2018). Demonstrated that unsupervised pre-training on text followed by fine-tuning could achieve strong NLP performance.

nodes.label: GPT-3 (2020)
nodes.summary: OpenAI's 175-billion parameter model (2020) demonstrated emergent few-shot learning abilities. Showed that scaling language models yields qualitatively new capabilities.

nodes.label: GPT-4 (2023)
nodes.summary: OpenAI's multimodal model released March 2023. Accepts text and images, passes professional exams (bar exam 90th percentile), and demonstrates broad reasoning abilities.

nodes.label: Vision Transformer / ViT (2020)
nodes.summary: Dosovitskiy et al. (2020) applied transformers directly to image patches, proving that pure attention without convolutions matches or beats CNNs on image classification.

nodes.label: Autoencoders
nodes.summary: Networks trained to reconstruct their input through a bottleneck, learning compressed representations. Foundational for dimensionality reduction and generative models.

nodes.label: Word2Vec (2013)
nodes.summary: Mikolov et al. (2013) at Google trained word embeddings using Skip-gram and CBOW. Showed that vector arithmetic captures semantic relationships (king - man + woman = queen).

nodes.label: Bayesian Networks
nodes.summary: Directed acyclic graphs representing conditional dependencies. Judea Pearl formalized them in the 1980s. Enable principled reasoning under uncertainty.

nodes.label: Hidden Markov Models
nodes.summary: Statistical models for sequential data with hidden states. Dominated speech recognition from the 1980s through the 2010s. Also used in bioinformatics and NLP.

nodes.label: Gaussian Processes
nodes.summary: Non-parametric Bayesian models defining distributions over functions. Provide uncertainty estimates naturally. Used in Bayesian optimization and surrogate modeling.

nodes.label: Markov Chain Monte Carlo
nodes.summary: Family of algorithms for sampling from probability distributions. Metropolis-Hastings (1953/1970) and Gibbs sampling enable Bayesian inference in complex models.

nodes.label: Variational Inference
nodes.summary: Approximate Bayesian inference by optimization rather than sampling. Turns intractable posterior computation into a tractable optimization problem. Faster than MCMC.

nodes.label: Topic Models (LDA)
nodes.summary: Latent Dirichlet Allocation (Blei et al., 2003) discovers latent topics in document collections using Bayesian generative models. Influential in text mining and NLP.

nodes.label: Probabilistic Programming
nodes.summary: Languages that combine programming with probabilistic modeling. Stan, Pyro, and NumPyro allow specifying generative models and performing automatic inference.

nodes.label: Causal Inference
nodes.summary: Judea Pearl's do-calculus and causal diagrams formalize reasoning about cause and effect beyond correlation. A bridge between statistics and interventional reasoning.

nodes.label: Q-Learning
nodes.summary: Watkins' 1989 model-free RL algorithm that learns action-value functions. Converges to optimal policy without requiring a model of the environment.

nodes.label: Policy Gradient Methods
nodes.summary: RL algorithms that directly optimize the policy by gradient ascent on expected reward. REINFORCE (Williams, 1992) and actor-critic methods are foundational variants.

nodes.label: Deep Reinforcement Learning
nodes.summary: Combining deep neural networks with RL. DeepMind's DQN (2013) played Atari from pixels. Enabled RL to scale to high-dimensional observation and action spaces.

nodes.label: AlphaGo (2016)
nodes.summary: DeepMind's AlphaGo defeated world champion Lee Sedol at Go in March 2016. Combined deep neural networks with Monte Carlo tree search. A landmark for AI.

nodes.label: AlphaStar (2019)
nodes.summary: DeepMind's AlphaStar reached Grandmaster level in StarCraft II in 2019, mastering a real-time strategy game with imperfect information and continuous action spaces.

nodes.label: RLHF (2017)
nodes.summary: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback, introduced by Christiano et al. (2017). Uses human preferences to define reward signals. Critical for aligning LLMs like ChatGPT and Claude.

nodes.label: Multi-Agent RL
nodes.summary: RL with multiple interacting agents that cooperate or compete. Self-play in AlphaGo Zero and multi-agent training in AlphaStar are landmark examples.

nodes.label: Model-Based RL
nodes.summary: RL methods that learn a model of environment dynamics and plan using it. More sample-efficient than model-free methods but requires accurate world models.

nodes.label: Generative Adversarial Networks (2014)
nodes.summary: Goodfellow et al. (2014) introduced adversarial training: a generator creates samples while a discriminator judges authenticity. Produced breakthrough image synthesis results.

nodes.label: Variational Autoencoders (2013)
nodes.summary: Kingma and Welling (2013) combined autoencoders with variational inference, creating a principled framework for learning latent representations and generating new samples.

nodes.label: Diffusion Models (2020)
nodes.summary: Ho et al.'s DDPM (2020) generates data by learning to reverse a noise diffusion process. Achieved image quality surpassing GANs with more stable training.

nodes.label: Stable Diffusion (2022)
nodes.summary: Stability AI's open-source latent diffusion model (2022). Performs diffusion in a compressed latent space, making high-quality image generation accessible on consumer GPUs.

nodes.label: DALL-E (2021)
nodes.summary: OpenAI's text-to-image model (January 2021) using a transformer to generate images from text descriptions. DALL-E 2 (2022) and DALL-E 3 (2023) dramatically improved quality.

nodes.label: Large Language Models
nodes.summary: Massive transformer-based models trained on internet-scale text corpora. Exhibit emergent abilities including reasoning, code generation, and instruction following.

nodes.label: ChatGPT (2022)
nodes.summary: OpenAI's conversational AI released November 2022. Fine-tuned GPT-3.5 with RLHF. Reached 100M users in 2 months, making AI mainstream worldwide.

nodes.label: Claude
nodes.summary: Anthropic's AI assistant, first released March 2023. Trained with Constitutional AI (RLAIF) for improved safety and helpfulness. Emphasizes harmlessness and honesty.

nodes.label: Image Generation
nodes.summary: The field of synthesizing images from noise, text, or other inputs. Evolved from GANs through VAEs to diffusion models, achieving photorealistic quality by 2022.

nodes.label: Text-to-Video Generation
nodes.summary: Generating video from text descriptions. OpenAI's Sora (2024) and similar models extend diffusion to temporal consistency. One of the most challenging generative tasks.

nodes.label: Genetic Algorithms
nodes.summary: John Holland's 1975 optimization method inspired by natural selection. Solutions evolve through selection, crossover, and mutation over generations.

nodes.label: Genetic Programming
nodes.summary: Evolving computer programs represented as tree structures. John Koza (1992) pioneered evolving LISP programs to solve symbolic regression and classification problems.

nodes.label: Neuroevolution
nodes.summary: Evolving neural network architectures and weights. NEAT (Stanley, 2002) co-evolves topology and weights. OpenAI showed evolution can match RL for policy search (2017).

nodes.label: Swarm Intelligence
nodes.summary: Collective behavior of decentralized agents. Ant Colony Optimization (Dorigo, 1992) and Particle Swarm Optimization (Kennedy & Eberhart, 1995) solve combinatorial problems.

nodes.label: Evolutionary Strategies
nodes.summary: Black-box optimization using population-based search with Gaussian perturbations. CMA-ES (Hansen, 2001) is the gold standard. Competitive with RL for policy optimization.

nodes.label: Computer Vision
nodes.summary: Enabling machines to interpret visual information. From edge detection to CNNs to vision transformers. Applications in autonomous driving, medical imaging, and AR.

nodes.label: Natural Language Processing
nodes.summary: AI for understanding and generating human language. Evolved from rule-based systems through statistical methods to transformer-based models that achieve near-human performance.

nodes.label: Speech Recognition
nodes.summary: Converting spoken language to text. Evolved from HMMs to deep learning. OpenAI's Whisper (2022) provides robust multilingual transcription as an open model.

nodes.label: Robotics
nodes.summary: Integrating AI with physical systems. Combines computer vision, RL, and planning for manipulation, locomotion, and human-robot interaction.

nodes.label: Recommendation Systems
nodes.summary: Algorithms predicting user preferences. Collaborative filtering, content-based filtering, and deep learning approaches power Netflix, Spotify, YouTube, and Amazon recommendations.

nodes.label: Autonomous Vehicles
nodes.summary: Self-driving cars combining computer vision, sensor fusion, and planning. Waymo, Tesla, and Cruise represent different approaches (LiDAR+HD maps vs pure vision).

nodes.label: AI Drug Discovery
nodes.summary: Using ML to accelerate pharmaceutical research. Molecular property prediction, generative chemistry, and virtual screening reduce time and cost of drug development.

nodes.label: Protein Folding (AlphaFold)
nodes.summary: DeepMind's AlphaFold2 (2020) solved protein structure prediction with atomic accuracy. Won CASP14 by a massive margin. Released structures for 200M+ proteins.
